coredata.dc:description: This work pacts with a simple and environmentally friendly hydrothermal method to synthesize clove buds-derived carbon dots (CCDs) at different temperatures. Five CCDs samples were synthesized at different reaction temperatures ranging from 120 °C to 200 °C, respectively. Using UV–Visible (UV–Vis) and fluorescence spectroscopy (FL), the optical characteristics of CCDs made at various temperatures were examined and compared. The results show that the optical properties of prepared carbon dots (CDs) are significantly influenced by the temperature of carbonization. The cytocompatibility and hemolysis studies of optimized CCDs were performed to understand their interaction in the biological environment. The free radical scavenging capability of CDs was also explored. Further, in vitro cellular imaging of optimized CCDs200 was performed which shows strong and multicolour emission when excited with different laser sources. Interestingly, CDs are capable of brightening up RNA-rich nucleoli. Testing using ribonuclease digestion validates the localization of the CCDs in the nucleolus. Further, intracellular stability and good counterstaining compatibility feature them as a promising fluorescence probe for nucleolus imaging. Therefore, we have explored medicinal plant fruits as a carbon precursor for the synthesis of CDs without using any modifier which can be a potential candidate for multicolour nucleolus imaging and free radical scavenging applications.

head.ce:title: Effect of reaction temperatures on optical properties of clove buds derived carbon dots for targeting nucleolus

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work pacts with a simple and environmentally friendly hydrothermal method to synthesize clove buds-derived carbon dots (CCDs) at different temperatures. Five CCDs samples were synthesized at different reaction temperatures ranging from 120 °C to 200 °C, respectively. Using UV–Visible (UV–Vis) and fluorescence spectroscopy (FL), the optical characteristics of CCDs made at various temperatures were examined and compared. The results show that the optical properties of prepared carbon dots (CDs) are significantly influenced by the temperature of carbonization. The cytocompatibility and hemolysis studies of optimized CCDs were performed to understand their interaction in the biological environment. The free radical scavenging capability of CDs was also explored. Further, in vitro cellular imaging of optimized CCDs200 was performed which shows strong and multicolour emission when excited with different laser sources. Interestingly, CDs are capable of brightening up RNA-rich nucleoli. Testing using ribonuclease digestion validates the localization of the CCDs in the nucleolus. Further, intracellular stability and good counterstaining compatibility feature them as a promising fluorescence probe for nucleolus imaging. Therefore, we have explored medicinal plant fruits as a carbon precursor for the synthesis of CDs without using any modifier which can be a potential candidate for multicolour nucleolus imaging and free radical scavenging applications.

ce:section.ce:para: The nucleolus is an important compartment of the cell known as the brain of the nucleus. It plays a vital role in ribosomal RNA (rRNA) synthesis and process, transcription (DNA copied to mRNA), and messenger RNA (mRNA) translation (directs protein synthesis) [1,2]. Cancer, a deadly genetic disease, does not show any symptoms at its early stage. Clinical diagnosis using conventional devices like X-rays Computed Tomography (CT) and Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) are not capable of showing any change in the morphology at an early stage. Under physiological and pathological conditions nucleolar dysmorphology has been taken as one of the hallmarks of diagnosing cancer progression depending on its changes in shape, size, and morphology [2,3]. Imaging of nucleolus always remains a difficult endeavor due to the lack of specific targets. SYTO RNASelect is a commercially available organic dye used for staining nucleolus or tracking the morphological change of nucleolus. Nevertheless, its applicability for biomedical applications is limited because of high cost, tedious staining steps, and more specificity toward the fixed cell [4,5]. In the last few years, a significant effort has been made by researchers for the expansion of novel nucleolus probes with additional features to encounter the above-mentioned limitations. In this framework, a range of organic/inorganic molecular dyes [6–10], metal nanoclusters [11,12], and metal-coordinated complexes [13,14] or hybrids [15–17] have been developed for targeting nucleolus inside cells [18]. However, their applications in biomedical fields are still limited due to complex or multi-step synthesis stages, photobleaching tendency, poor functional group selectivity, and inorganic/organic precursors-based toxicity [18,19]. Therefore, a quest for cost-effective, non-toxic, and environmentally friendly fluorescent nanomaterial with good photobleaching resistance is needed for specific targeting of nucleolus.

In this regard, carbon dots (CDs) as member of carbonaceous nanomaterials are well known for their small size (>=10 nm), excitation-dependent/independent fluorescence emission, good water dispersity/solubility, photobleaching resistance, easy surface group tunability, minimal toxicity. These distinct features make them a potential candidate in biological applications compared to other organic/inorganic nanomaterials. CDs are potential fluorescent probes for staining or targeting various cellular organelles due to their easily adjustable surface and size. But still, there are very few reports available on CDs mediated nucleolus targeting or staining. Among them, researchers have used either chemical (citric acid [3,20–22], ascorbic acid [23], polyethylene glycol [24], phenylenediamine [25–27], 2,4-dihydroxy benzaldehyde and 2,3-dimethyl benzothiazole iodide [2]) or natural chemical mixed (cow manure and ethylenediamine [28]) sources of carbon precursors for CDs synthesis. The advantages of CDs derived from natural precursors include their low cost, low toxicity, easy availability, and less processing complexity [29]. Clove buds, a fruit part of the medicinal clove plant might be a good precursor choice for CDs synthesis due to their known good biological activity [30]. As per our knowledge, there are no reports on the synthesis of CDs using a simple synthesis route utilizing only natural precursors without modification for nucleolus imaging application.

In this work, we have reported an easy-to-use, low-cost, and environmentally friendly material and method for the synthesis of CDs utilizing medicinal plant fruit buds (clove). Five sets of CCDs are synthesized at different reaction temperatures ranging from 120 to 200 °C with a 20 °C gap. It is revealed that the optical properties of CDs are more positively impacted at higher reaction temperatures. The cytocompatibility and hemolysis studies of optimized CCDs are performed to understand their interaction in the biological environment. The pH stability, photostability, and antioxidant activity are also investigated. Further, in vitro cellular imaging of optimized CDs produced at 200 °C is performed which shows strong and multicolour emission of CDs upon excitation with different laser sources. Interestingly, CDs are capable of glowing RNA-rich nucleoli which are further investigated using RNase digestion experiments. Along with that, their intracellular stability and counterstain capability are also tested. Therefore, we have explored medicinal plant fruits as a carbon precursor for the synthesis of CDs without consuming any external oxidizing agent which has been proven as a potential candidate in the fields of multicolour nucleolus imaging and free radical scavenging.

Clove buds were purchased from the supermarket of the IIT Kharagpur campus in West Bengal, which was previously mentioned in the report [31]. Some chemicals for instance dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), DNase/RNase, and 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-Diphenyltetrazolium Bromide (98 %) (MTT), purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. The National Centre for Cell Science (NCCS), located in Pune, India, provided the MG-63 and 3T3 cells. High-glucose Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were acquired from Thermo Fischer Scientific, USA. SYTO RNASelect was procured from Molecular Probes. Throughout the study, double distilled water was used as the solvent medium.

Clove-derived carbon dots (CCDs) were synthesized following a modified method of a previously reported article [31]. Briefly, 50 mL of water and 1 g of powdered clove were combined in a beaker and stirred continuously for 2 h. Then whole solution was shifted to a 100 mL capacity hydrothermal autoclave reactor and heated for 5 h at different temperatures ranging between 120–200 °C with a gap of 20 °C, respectively, in the hot air oven. The name of samples was denoted as CCDs120, CCDs140, CCDs160, CCDs180, and CCDs200 according to their synthesis temperature of 120 °C, 140 °C, 160 °C, 180 °C, and 200 °C, respectively. To acquire purified fluorescent CCDs, centrifugation (at 10000 rpm for 15 min.) and filtration (0.22-µm filter membrane) were conducted after cooling the hydrothermal reactor at room temperature. For later usage, the resulting water-dispersed CCDs were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C.

The UV–Visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy (MOTRAS Scientific, India) was used to analyze the absorption spectra of CDs with a cuvette path length of 1 cm. The functional groups included in CDs were examined using Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), which involved drop-casting, drying, and measuring the samples on KBr pellets in the 4,000–500 cm−1 wavenumber range using a Nicolet 6700 (Thermo Fischer Scientific Instruments, USA). The fluorescence study was analyzed using a Horiba Jobin Yvon Fluoromax-4 spectrofluorometer. The nanostructure of synthesized CCDs was analyzed using High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM, Thermo Scientific, Talos F200X G2). Using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å), the X′Pert Pro high-resolution X-ray diffractometer (Philips PANanalytical, Netherlands) was used to accomplish the X-ray diffraction measurement in the region of 2θ angle 10°− 70°. All in vitro cell images were captured using a microscope (Axio Observer Z1) in bright field and fluorescence mode. The zeta potential of CDs was calculated using the Particle size & Zeta potential analyzer model Nano Partica, SZ-100 (Horiba Scientific, Japan). The alpha 300R Raman spectrometer (Witec, Germany) was used to get the Raman spectra. An LED laser source operating at 532 nm stimulated the gel matrix surface. For elemental composition profiling and antioxidant study of CDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI 5000 Verse Probe III (ULVAC-PHI Inc., Japan)) and Electron Paramagnetic Resonance (EPR, Bruker A300 spectrometer) were performed as well.

Complete DMEM media, which included 10 % FBS, 1 % antimycotic-antibacterial, and incomplete high glucose media, was used to cultivate MG-63 and 3T3 cells. The cells were sown in 12 well culture plates over the coverslips and placed inside an incubator maintained at 37 °C, 5 % CO2, and 95 % humidity for 24 h to grow.

The MG 63 cells and 3T3 cells were grown on two separate 12-well culture plates containing coverslip. The developed cells underwent a 12 h incubation period with CCDs treatment. Following that, samples were thrown away, and cells were observed under a fluorescence microscope after being twice cleaned with PBS buffer solution. In the fixed cell experiment, cells after PBS washing were treated with pre-chilled methanol (−20 °C) for 3 min for fixation. The coverslips were imaged using brightfield and fluorescence mode of microscope. For comparison commercialized dye, SYTO RNASelect (5 mM in DMSO) was also used as a staining agent. The counterstain capability was investigated using DAPI staining on CCDs-treated prefixed cells before imaging. The pH-dependent fluorescence responses of CDs under acidic, neutral, and basic conditions were evaluated by using the MG-63 cell line as a model. MG-63 overnight seeded cells were treated with CDs solution in media tuned at different pH in the range 5–8 with an incubation time of 24 h.

These experiments were carried out to validatethat CCDs are located in RNA-rich nucleoli. In brief, 3T3 cells that had been previously stained with CCDs and fixed with pre-chilled methanol were permeabilized for 20 min using Triton-X-100and then treated with BSA. Images were taken after 2 h of treatment with RNase (50 µg/mL) and DNase (50 µg/mL), and PBS washing. Additionally, spectroscopic methods were used to assess any modifications in the optical characteristics of CCDs following their interaction with DNA or RNA. During the experiment, CDs without DNA or RNA was taken as a control.

The cytotoxicity test of CCDs was performed on both cell lines using a colorimetric MTT assay. In summary, each of the two cell types was seeded separately at a density of 5000 cells per well in 96-well plates. Subsequently, CCDs were introduced to wells at varying doses (100–1100 µg/mL) and incubated for a full day. Wells in triplicate were utilized for each concentration and control test. When the times were up, the media was taken out, and well plates were then filled with 180 µL PBS and 20 µL of MTT (5 mg/mL), which were left for 4 h more in an oven at 37 °C. Then MTT was removed and 100 µL of DMSO was put in the well plates and mixed well using a shaker, before taking optical density (OD) reading at 570 nm wavelength by a microplate reader (Byonoy Absorbance 96, Germany). The cell viability was calculated using the formulae:

Heparin sodium/sodium citrate (1:9) vacutainers with a 5 mL capacity were used to collect fresh human blood. Centrifuging the blood for 15 min at 4000 rpm produced the RBC pallet. After being cleaned with 1 % Phosphate Buffer Saline (PBS), the packed RBC suspension was diluted by applying 25 mL of PBS [32]. Test samples included 0.2 mL of the sample suspension and 0.8 mL of PBS-RBC suspension. 0.2 mL of diluted blood was used to create the positive control and 0.8 mL of deionized water was used to create the negative control. Wells in triplicate were utilized for each concentration, positive control, and negative control test. After 3 h of incubation at 37 °C, each tube was centrifuged for 10 min at 5000 rpm. Using a plate reader, the supernatant solution's OD was measured at 545 nm. The following formula was used to determine the percentage of hemolysis:

Here, ODsample = Absorbance of test samples, ODPc = Absorbance of positive controls, and ODNc = Absorbance of negative controls.

A DPPH assay was used to assess the antioxidant activity of CCDs, following the procedure outlined in our previously published study [31]. In brief different concentrations of CCDs were added in 3 mL of methanol solvent, treated with 0.3 mL of DPPH (0.5 mM), and then the mixture was incubated for 100 min in a dark environment. Methanol with CCDs was employed as the correction factor, and DPPH without CCDs was utilized as the control. A UV–Vis spectrophotometer was used to record the OD at a wavelength of 517 nm. Measurements were replicated three times with seven concentrations of CCDs and a control to ensure reproducibility. The antioxidant efficacy against the DPPH free radical was calculated using the following equation:

Here, Acorrection is the absorbance of CCDs in methanol without DPPH, Acontrol is the absorbance without CCDs addition, and ACCDs is the absorbance of tested samples at different concentrations.

Intracellular ROS were detected using a ROS assay kit. Cells in six-well plates were exposed to H2O2 (200 µM) for 6 h with and without a sample. Following a 20-minute treatment with DCFH-DA (10 µM) at 37 °C, they were twice cleaned with PBS before being photographed under a microscope. The untreated cells were used as a negative control.

Standard deviation ± mean (n = 3) was used to express data from cytocompatibility, hemolysis, and antioxidant tests. To confirm differences across treatment procedures, One-way Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) was utilized [33], and p-values less than 0.01 were deemed significant. GraphPad Prism was used to evaluate the results.

The clove-derived CDs (CCDs) were synthesized at different temperatures ranging between 120–200 °C at a 20 °C interval under hydrothermal treatment with 5 h holding time in each case. As the carbonization temperature increased, CDs are seen to transform from dark brown to light yellow in colour, as shown in Fig S1 . Similarly, under exposure to UV light, the brightest green colour appearance is witnessed for CDs carbonized at the highest temperature of 200 °C. This indicates that carbonization at high temperatures resulted in CDs with high fluorescence intensity. For additional analysis, every sample was kept at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of reaction temperature on optical properties of CCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of CDs at varying reaction temperatures are underpinned by several mechanisms including changes in particle size, surface functional groups, degree of carbonization, and quantum confinement effect. Therefore, UV–Vis, fluorescence spectroscopy, FTIR, HR-TEM, and Raman were among the extensive characterization methods used to evaluate the effect of synthesis temperature on CDs' characteristics.

The optical properties of CDs synthesized at different temperatures are studied using UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. The absorption spectra and bandgaps of all five samples, each produced at a distinct reaction temperature, are displayed in Fig. 1 a and Fig. S2 . Structurally, CDs have a core-shell structure consisting of sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon network. The core is created by stacking many graphene pieces in an ordered/disordered fashion responsible for π-π* electron transition, and the shell consists of functional groups dispersed throughout their surface is accountable for n- π* electron transition [21,34,35]. At a reaction temperature of 120 °C, CCDs120 shows three absorption peaks at wavelengths of 255, 276, and 365 nm. The alkene bonds and their conjugates are evidenced through π-π* electron transition associated with absorption in the 240–290 nm range, while the existence of a carbonyl peak (n- π* electron transition) is evidenced with a broad hump centred at 365 nm [36]. Similarly, the direct bandgaps calculated from UV–Vis data show values of 4.17 eV, 4.16 eV, 4.14 eV, 4.11 eV, and 4.148 eV, on increasing temperature from 120-200 °C, simultaneously. The increase in reaction temperatures from 120 °C to 140 °C, CCDs140 shows no significant change in absorption intensity and peaks, with a negligible change in the bandgap, as shown in Fig. S2 . While, at a reaction temperature of 160 °C, an increase in absorption spectrum intensity without significant change in their peak positions is noticed for the CCDs160 sample with a decrease in its bandgap energy. Interestingly, at a reaction temperature of 180 °C, the absorption peak at 255 nm seems to change and the peak at 276 nm is found to be more intense. This indicates an increase in core size either due to a rise in the degree of graphitization or the conjugation of graphitic fragments attached with sp2 hybridized carbon, which also shows a further decrease in the bandgap [37]. While at a temperature of 200 °C, an abrupt disappearance of the peak at 255 nm with an increase in bandgap is observed, in line with previously reported findings [31]. The disappearance of the 255 nm peak that belongs to alkene bonds and their conjugates (π-π* electron transition) might be due to the breaking and merging of two graphitic fragments in the core, resulting in the increase of bandgap at the highest carbonization temperature in the experiment. It is evident that when the carbonization temperature rose from 120 °C to 180 °C, the peak's intensity progressively increased. While at carbonization of 200 °C, the intensity of peaks decreased, similarly reported by Sarkar et al. [34]. They mentioned that smaller CDs are attached to the surface of larger CDs, after carbonization at higher temperatures these larger CDs vanishes resulting in a decrease of peak intensity.

Fluorescence spectroscopy (FL) is used to analyze the effect of carbonization temperatures on the photoluminescence properties of all samples. The emission behavior of CDs synthesized at various temperatures for a 5 h reaction time is shown in Fig. 2 a, 2c, 2e, and 2g. Every sample exhibits wavelength-dependent emission behavior upon changing the excitation wavelength from 340 to 520 nm with an increment of 20 nm. This signifies that the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs are dependent on both core (quantum confinement) and surface state (oxide-related surface effect) [38]. Therefore, the effect of reaction temperatures on the fluorescence behavior of CCDs is studied. At a reaction temperature of 120 °C, the fluorescence spectrum of CCDs120 shows the highest emission at 465 nm when excited with a wavelength of 380 nm, as depicted in Fig. S3 . On the other hand, by increasing the reaction temperature to 140 °C and 160 °C, a decrease in both excitation and emission wavelengths is experienced with an increase in fluorescence intensity. CCDs140 and CCDs160 exhibit maximum fluorescence at 444 nm and 439 nm when excited at 360 nm wavelength. Fig. S2b-d indicates, first an increase and then a decrease in the stock shift from changing temperature from 120-160 °C because of continuous change in surface groups as a result of an increase in the degree of carbonization. More intriguingly, at a reaction temperature of 180 °C, CCDs180 exhibits maximum fluorescence at 485 nm on excitation at 400 nm. This random increase in both excitation and emission wavelength might be due to either an increase in core structure (more graphitization) or radiative surface group (increased stokes shift) [38]. Further carbonization at 200 °C, CCDs200 shows a sudden decrease in excitation (360 nm) and emission (434 nm) wavelength with an abrupt increase in fluorescence intensity. This indicates carbonization at 200 °C is beneficial to the formation of a defined core through dehydration/carbonization [39] of raw materials and reduction of surface defects of prepared CDs with an increase in the fluorescence intensity.

Fig. 2Sa shows that at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, a blue shift in emission spectra is observed on increasing carbonization temperature. This signifies the breaking of larger CDs particles into smaller particles with higher fluorescence or a higher population of sp2 hybridized carbon on increasing carbonization temperature that was further supported by HR-TEM and FTIR analysis. The emission is observed in the 430–650 nm region for the majority of the samples, as shown in Fig. 2b, 2d, 2f, and 2h. Interestingly, all samples show nearly equal emission peak shifts upon excitation in the 340 nm to 380 nm range. When stimulated with a wavelength above 400 nm, the sample with the highest intensity in FL characteristics exhibits a red shift and a progressive decline in intensity.

There are findings indicating that emission at longer wavelengths (green or red region) is ascribed to surface defect states, while the emission at lower wavelengths (blue region) is caused by electron-hole recombination or the quantum size effect [25]. The FL property of all five samples clearly shows that when the excitation wavelength changes, specific trap states become active and affect the emission [40]. Therefore, the presence of sp2 carbon core, quantum confinement, and the existence of various trap states on the surface of the CDs can be attributed to the intriguing photoluminescence property of the CDs [12,29]. Strong fluorescence intensity of CDs can be utilized for targetting and imaging various organelles inside the cells to understand their mechanism/metabolic activity. The optimized sample CCDs200 shows maximum fluorescence intensity that can be used for optoelectronic and bioimaging applications.

To investigate the impact of carbonization temperature on various functional groups and chemical bonds linked to CDs, FTIR spectroscopy was conducted on all five samples. Aqueous extract of clove buds contains various phytochemicals rich in allyl, hydroxyl, and methoxy functional groups [41,42]. From the stretching and deformation vibrations of the O–H group, all CCDs samples show a widened absorption peak in the region of 3600–3200 cm−1 and two minor peaks in the range of 1400–1435 cm−1 (C-OH in plane blend) and 700–900 cm−1 (out plane aromatic C–H blend) [43]. A drop in the sample processed at 200 °C at a wavenumber of 3443 cm−1 is observed, and a progressive increase in transmittance intensity up to 180 °C is noted with a gradual increase in reaction temperature from 120 to 200 °C. The –CH3 and –CH2 groups' symmetric and asymmetric vibrations are represented by two little peaks located at 2922 cm−1 and 2862 cm−1, respectively [31]. The peak at 1050 cm−1 can be ascribed to the CD's surface stretching vibration bands of C–O. As shown in Fig. 1b, an increase in carbonization temperature from 120-200 °C, results in an increase in peak intensity at wavenumber 1630 cm−1 and 1735 cm−1 belonging to C=C and C=O bonds. This suggests that the phytochemicals present in cloves are destroyed to form more sp2 and sp3 hydridized carbon during a slow increase in hydrothermal carbonization might be the reason for the increase of C=C and C=O bonds peak at increasing temperatures. This leads to the formation of a more defined core and effective functional groups at high temperatures.

Using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), the surface chemical state and composition of CCDs200 were examined. XPS spectrum shows carbon–oxygen fingerprints in the CCDs200 sample. The presence of different atomic bonds is dictated by the high-resolution spectra of C 1s and O 1s, as displayed in Fig. S4a and 4b. The amount of sp2 hybridized carbon is indicated by the existence of C–C and C = C bonds, while sp3 is produced by functional groups that contain oxygen. Concerning their respective binding energies of 284.6 eV, 285.8 eV, and 288.3 eV, the deconvoluted C 1s spectra demonstrate the existence of C=C, C-O-C, and C=O bonds [44,45] Additionally, the deconvoluted O 1s spectra are displayed in Fig. S4b, where two peaks corresponding to C = O and C-O bonds are located at 531.2 eV and 532.4 eV [45,46], respectively. The presence of all these chemical bonds is in support of the FTIR result. The above study suggested that functional groups such as −COOH and −OH might be present in the synthesized CDs. The XPS high-resolution data is used to compute the sp2 fraction. This is accomplished by calculating the ratio between the overall area under the C 1s XPS spectra and the integrated area of the peaks that correspond to the sp2 (C–C/C=C) and other oxygen-containing functional groups (C=O, C-O) [47]. The calculated percentages of the fraction for C–C/C = C, C-O, and C=O bonds are 37.5 %, 27.5 %, and 35 %, respectively. This indicates the existence of sp2 and sp3 hybridized core–shell structures of CDs. The functional groups over CDs surface are responsible for good solubility and bioconjugation enabling them to be conjugated with biomolecules or ligands specific to the nucleolus, improving the specificity and targeting efficacy of CDs when targeting the nucleolus [48,49].

A crystal structure analysis using X-ray diffraction has been conducted to examine the synthesized CDs, as shown in Fig. S5a. The XRD spectra of all samples hydrothermally treated at different temperatures represent one broader peak with a 2θ value of 24.2°, which corresponds to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon [31,44]. According to XRD data, the intensity peak is increased as the temperature of the hydrothermal increases might be due to a decrease in disordered carbon atoms [50].

Fig. S5b shows the Raman spectra of all CCD samples synthesized at different reaction temperatures. The disordered (D band) and graphite (G band) carbon in carbon materials are represented by the Raman spectra's peaks at 1347 cm−1 and 1564 cm−1, respectively [34,36]. On increasing reaction temperature from 120 to 200 °C, the ID/IG ratios are found to decrease. With their higher preparation temperatures, the CDs' greater conjugated sp2 domain or the number of embedded graphitic carbon atoms is also implied by the lower ID/IG value.

The HR-TEM images as shown in Fig. 3 show all CDs possess a nearly spherical shape and changes in carbonization temperature have a direct impact on their average diameter. The size of the CCDs140 (Fig. 3 a, 3d) shows an aggregation of functional group-rich particles to form a large sphere. The smallest and largest particles show a diameter of 7.5 nm and 31 nm, while sample CCDs180 (Fig. 3b, 3e) has a combination of functional group-rich particles along with smaller carbonized particles. Larger-sized, partly crystalline, and agglomerated particles are seen in the CCDs180 sample [38]. After carbonization at high temperatures, CCDs200 (Fig. 3c) show small-size (average diameter 2 to 6 nm), crystalline particles with little defined cores (sp2 hybridized) and functional groups (sp3 hybridized) along with a decrease in the degree of agglomeration. DLS data represent the hydrodynamic size of 35.6 nm, 19.3 nm, and 8.7 nm for CCDs140, CCDs180, and CCDs200 samples, respectively, in a similar line. This indicates a lower degree of agglomeration in the CCDs200 sample compared to others. The larger hydrodynamic size of CCDs180 signifies the agglomeration of aggregates containing smaller particles sitting over the surface of larger particles. These results corroborate FL, bandgap, and UV–Vis data that show a transition in the size of CDs upon rising temperature. This suggests that the size, crystallinity, and surface characteristics of CDs are significantly influenced by the reaction temperature during synthesis. The CCDs200 sample's tiny size, well-defined core, and effective surface group also validated by Raman spectroscopy make them more promising for use in the investigation of cellular imaging applications. These studies indicate a significant effect of reaction temperature on the optical properties of prepared CDs. This made it possible to comprehend in great depth how temperature affects surface functional groups, particle size, and optical characteristics. The CDs synthesized at 200 °C shows a small size, effective functional groups, uniform dispersion, and strong fluorescence emission intensity compared to other temperatures prepared CDs. Therefore, the CCDs200 sample was further investigated for imaging applications.

An examination of the effects of pH and UV exposure on CDs was carried out to understand stability. As shown in Fig. 4 a-d, variations in the absorption and fluorescence spectra are seen upon altering the pH values and UV light exposure time. Fascinatingly, the fluorescence spectra exhibit a good, bright band at pH 7, indicating that they are acceptable for use in cellular imaging. CCDs exhibit strong fluorescence and structural integrity at both acidic and neutral pH values, which makes them appropriate for bioimaging applications in a variety of biological contexts, such as acidic tumor microenvironments and neutral physiological circumstances. The loss of fluorescence at basic pH is observed. The possible reason behind the pH-responsiveness of CDs is the protonation and deprotonation of functional groups on their surface at different pH values.

Additionally, the effect of bleaching on CCDs has been evaluated after UV exposure, and the results show that, for a maximum of 4 h, there is no significant shift in the peak intensity and location of the absorption and fluorescence spectra. This shows that prepared CCDs, which are protected against photobleaching and photodegradation by their strong core structure and efficient surface passivation, enable long-term continuous monitoring of cellular processes without appreciable signal loss. Thus, it can be said that under the investigated conditions, following UV exposure, CCDs do not congregate and do not appreciably decrease their intensity.

Cytocompatibility study and measurement of EC50 are important parameters for every material to be explored in biomedical applications. To assess the possible cytotoxicity of the as-synthesized CCDs, MG-63 and 3T3 cells are used in an MTT experiment. After the combination was incubated at various concentrations ranging from 0.1 to 1.1 mg/mL for a duration of 24 h, the cell survival of both cell lines against CCDs was determined.

After being treated with a CCDs solution at a dosage of 0.3 mg/mL for 24 h, Fig. 4e demonstrates that the cell viability of CCDs is still more than 80 % for both cell lines. According to these findings, the CCDs200 exhibits low cytotoxicity toward both cell lines, even at relatively high concentrations of up to 300 μg/mL, which is significantly higher than the concentration required for cellular imaging (i.e., 50 μg/mL). It is even more than 50 % at a higher concentration of 0.7 mg/mL. Compared to other published organic/inorganic or carbon dots-based fluorescent probes for nucleolus imaging applications, this illustrates the low toxicity of synthetic CCDs [3,14,15,25,26]. So, the synthesized CCDs can be used as a potent candidate for biomedical applications due to their excellent cytocompatibility even at higher dosages.

Hemolysis is the term for hemoglobin being released from red blood cells (RBCs) and denotes a disruption in the integrity of the red blood cell membrane. Hemolysis, like RBC shape, is a reflection of how biomaterials interact with the RBC membrane [51]. The hemolysis percentage of RBCs subjected to various CCDs200 concentrations and up to 3 h of incubation are displayed in Fig. 4f. When compared to the PBS control, RBCs at lower sample concentrations did not lyse even after 3 h of incubation. However, at a concentration of 2 mg/mL of CDs, 4 % of the RBCs are lysed. Hemolysis > 5 % indicates that the sample begins to impair red blood cells, according to the ASTM E2524-08 standard [ASTM. “Standard Test Method for Analysis of Hemolytic Properties of Nanoparticles.” (2013)]. These results signify that CCDs200 have biosafety promises at higher concentrations below 1 mg/mL.

A DPPH free radical assay, one of the primary techniques used to measure the antioxidant efficacy of as-synthesized CCDs200, was employed. The addition of CDs decolourized DPPH from purple to yellow due to its easy hydrogen-giving capability. The antioxidant activity of CCDs increased with increasing concentration representing its dose-dependent response, where the concentration of 500 µg/mL or higher results in maximum antioxidant activity reaching 98 %. The result as shown in Fig. 5 a, is used to calculate the EC50 value of CCDs which is found to be 0.067 mg/mL, which is the concentration needed to decrease 50 % of the initial DPPH free radical. CCDs with a lower EC50 value are more effective at scavenging DPPH free radicals. As indicated in Table S1 , CCDs exhibit better antioxidant activity and lower EC50 value against DPPH radical degradation when compared to other reported CDs at their highest reported doses. As illustrated in Fig. 5b, the absorption peaks of DPPH radicals at 517 nm gradually diminish as the concentration of CCDs increases from 0.030 to 0.5 mg/mL in the reaction mixture. Additionally, utilizing electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR), the electron donating/accepting capacity of CCDs is investigated over the DPPH free radical. EPR spectra in Fig. 5c show a decrease in signal intensity of DPPH radical when the concentration of CCDs increased. This is due to the stabilization of DPPH radicals into diamagnetic molecules after accepting hydrogen or electrons at the divalent nitrogen atom site. The intracellular ROS scavenging capability of CCDs is also investigated using a DCFDA kit. As shown in Fig. 5d, MG-63 cells treated with H2O2 but without CCDs are considered positive control, whereas untreated cells are considered negative control

The H2O2-treated cells with the highest and lowest concentrations of CCDs are test samples. After treatment with DCFDA assay for 20 min, it can be observed that the fluorescence signal due to ROS formation is found to be lower and similar to the negative control representing the ROS scavenging capability of CCDs. The results indicate the efficient antioxidant activity of CCDs can be coupled with imaging capabilities to create theranostic platforms that enable real-time disease monitoring and treatment. During cancer treatment, CDs' capacity to scavenge ROS can be used to shield healthy cells from oxidative harm. Moreover, CDs can be combined with anticancer medications to maximize their therapeutic benefit and reduce their adverse effects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Live/fixed cell nucleolus imaging of CCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The efficient fluorescence, small size (2–6 nm), good photostability, and cytocompatibility of as-prepared CCDs200 inspired us to investigate its imaging capability using MG-63 live cells as a model. The cells are incubated for 12 h at 37 °C after treatment with CCDs, and then images are captured utilizing a fluorescence microscope. Fig. 6 , shows the fluorescence and bright field merged imaging of MG-63 live cells. The merge images of bright fields along with fluorescence images and their insets indicate the accumulation of CCDs more specifically in the nucleolus region. Along with that intensity, profiles represent the highest fluorescence intensity for the AF-546 channel followed by AF-480 and Texas-Red channels, showing its multicolour emission characteristics.

The CCDs display fluorescence emission under irradiation with three different laser sources, Alexa Fluro-480 (AF-480) excited with a wavelength of 493 nm, Alexa Fluoro-546 (AF-546) excited with a wavelength of 557 nm, and Texas-Red excited with a wavelength of 592 nm, respectively. There is a slight fluorescence that can also be seen in the cytoplasmic area and around the nuclear membrane region which might be due to the presence of RNA-rich ribosomal units in the cytoplasm. Further, by using fluorescence microscopy, multicolour fluorescence images of MG-63 and 3T3 fixed cells treated with CCDs200 are also captured, which show high-intensity fluorescence profiles for the nucleolus than the cytoplasmic area (Fig. 7 ). The fluorescence image with more intensity for MG-63 cells in comparison to 3T3 cells might be due to more nucleolar dysmorphology of cancer cells. Their co-localization with markers specific to the nucleolus (e.g., SYTO RNASelect), the imaging data showed that CDs selectively accumulate in the nucleolus [18], as shown in Fig. S6 .

Its comparable structure and localization imply that CCDs have been successfully positioned in the nucleolar areas. There is a minor fluorescence in the cytoplasmic area might be due to the tagging of CCDs with ribosomal RNA present inside ribosomes or might be due to the fixation of cells [28].

The brain of the nucleus, or nucleolus, is often referred to as the ribosomal RNA (rRNA) synthesis factory. Additionally, some DNAs can also be found in nucleoli. However, the DNAs present in the nucleus are electrostatically bound with positively charged histones. To further investigate the localization of CCDs in the nucleoli region, permeabilized-fixed cells were individually treated with RNase (hydrolyzed RNA) and DNase (hydrolyzed DNA) solution followed by CCDs treatment for comparative analysis with untreated ones, as shown in Fig. 8 . Interestingly, CCDs possessed specific accumulation in nucleolus even after DNase treatment with insignificant change in intensity. Notably, the fluorescence intensity reduced significantly in the nucleolus region after RNase treatment, signifying the RNA-rich nucleolus-targeting capability of CCDs. To further unveilthe nucleolus-targeting mechanism, the UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscopic examination of CCDs during contact with DNA or RNA is investigated, as shown in Fig. S7 . When CDs interact with DNA or RNA in an aqueous solution, their UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra (stimulated at 360 nm wavelength) exhibit a notable rise in absorption and fluorescence spectra without changing in peak location, which may indicate a non-covalent interaction. It is evident from the variations in the CCDs' fluorescence and absorption intensities that they respond more strongly to RNA than to DNA. Furthermore, compared to pristine CDs, the fluorescence intensity of CDs attached to RNA or DNA is noticeably stronger. In the literature, a comparable occurrence was also documented [4,17].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on nucleolus fluorescence intensity with CCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The pH-dependent fluorescence response of CDs is an important factor representing their behavior in acidic, neural, and basic conditions. The pH used for the growth and development of both normal and cancer cells is mostly maintained at a pH range of 7.2 to 7.4 for cell culture experiments. Cancer cells are well known for their inclination towards depolarization behavior as compared to normal cells, where membrane depolarization causes an enhanced rate of proliferation. Tuning extracellular pH might be an important factor in influencing the depolarization of cells and affecting the localization of CDs within the cells. Therefore, it is advised to investigate how extracellular pH affects CDs localization under acidosis or alkalosis environments, as these may result in stress or apoptosis. Research on the nucleolus targeting ability of carbon dots (CDs) and how extracellular pH affects it, can be an exciting field with broad applications in targeted therapy, diagnostics, and bioimaging. The effect of different pH of CCDs suspended medium ranging from 5 to 8 on nucleolus fluorescence intensity was investigated over MG-63 cells, as shown in Fig. 9 . The fixed concentration of CDs tuned at different pH were mixed with media to acquire 1 mL final volume and pH of the solution is recorded. The fluorescence signals at different pH were calculated using Image-J software. The extracted fluorescent intensity is found to be good in pH ranges of 5 to 7. However, at basic pH values of CCDs (pH 8), a sudden drop in fluorescent intensity is observed, similar to pH stability-based fluorescence spectroscopy data. Significant morphological changes of cells at acidic pH might be due to extracellular acidosis-induced membrane depolarization responsible for low growth rate. Interestingly, the fluorescence signal from the nucleolus is still strong. There is a gradual drop in the fluorescence intensity at the nucleolus site as the extracellular pH is raised from 5 to 8. This suggests that CCDs can be promising for simultaneously detecting pH and targeting nucleolus on a single platform [49]. The enhanced fluorescence at acidic pH and a decreased fluorescence intensity at basic pH may be the result of functional groups protonation/deprotonation or the aggregation/de-aggregation behavior of CCDs under pH variation, like other reported CDs [52,53]. They have mentioned that the π-π* and n-π* transitions are affected by the protonation or deprotonation of the functional groups (–OH, >C = O, –COOH) during pH variations resulting in replenished or diminished valence band, which quenches the fluorescence from the CDs at basic pH. Furthermore, the surface charge of CDs is significantly influenced by the protonation or deprotonation of functional groups linked to their surface because of pH fluctuation, which might help to understand the underlying mechanism. However, there is still more work to be done to fully understand how CDs sense pH. The nucleolus is rich in rRNAs, a negatively charged biomolecule tending to bind positive charge nanomaterials by electrostatic forces. We determined the zeta potential (z) of CDs at various pH levels. The zeta potential notably drops from −0.2 to −5.7 mV when pH increases from 3 to 7, and at pH 9, it drops even further to −11.8 mV, as shown in Fig. S8 . Deprotonation of surface groups is inferred to be caused by increased zeta potential at alkaline pH. When the pH is changed from acidic to basic, the increase in the negative surface charge of CDs causes a decrease in binding to negatively charged nucleoli, which lowers the fluorescence intensity coming from the nucleoli due to lower interaction [23]. The ability of CDs with varying pH-dependent surface charges to target nucleoli is a complex subject that depends on how the intracellular environment and the CDs' surface chemistry interact. Because of their pH-responsive activity and surface charge heterogeneity, CDs with variable pH-dependent surface charges have differing nucleolus targeting efficiencies [20]. Improved nucleolus targeting is demonstrated by CDs with regulated surface charges that can display both positive and negative charges.Therefore, the pH change of CCDs has a significant effect on their surface charge, which might be a possible reason for their affinity toward nucleolus targeting.

Two important properties of CDs that improve their usefulness for nucleolus imaging applications are their photostability and counterstaining ability. Elevated photostability guarantees reliable and extended imaging, mitigates phototoxicity, and facilitates high-definition visualization of nucleolar dynamics. The ability to counterstain validates the targeting accuracy of CDs, enhances specificity and contrast, and permits multiplexed imaging. All of these characteristics combine to make CDs extremely valuable for accurate and thorough imaging of the nucleolus in both fixed and living cells, which advances our knowledge of nucleolar processes and their relevance to health and illness.

The capacity of a dye to remain photostable during prolonged light source irradiation is crucial for its use in cell imaging applications. The fluorescence characteristics of mostly organic molecules and metal-based fluorophores decayed during continuous excitation limiting its shelf life and long-term imaging capability. Regarding this, CDs have an advantage over the dye stated above in terms of low toxicity and strong photostability when used as fluorescent probes for nucleolus imaging. In most of the reports, commercialized dye SYTO RNASelect is considered a standard nucleolus imaging probe, and its features in terms of photostability are compared [4]. Therefore, the intracellular photostability of CCDs and commercialized dye SYTO RNASelect are investigated and compared, as shown in Fig. 10 a. Separate treatments are administered to permeabilized-fixed cells using CCDs and SYTO RNASelect dyes. After a fixed period of treatment, cells are captured using microscopy with a continuous snap of 25 cycles with a gap interval of 30 s. At the 25th scan, it can be observed from Fig. S9 that the CCDs have still more fluorescence intensity in comparison to SYTO RNASelect dye, better or comparable to other reported nucleolus imaging probes [6,7]. This indicates satisfactory intracellular photostability of CCDs can be used as a reliable carrier for tracking and targeting nucleoli with good photobleaching resistance.

To better understand the relationships between different organelles or macromolecules, cellular imaging would benefit from the simultaneous achievement of multitarget staining through the use of diverse probes with varying light-emitting ranges. Nucleolus tracker's compatibility with other conventional cellular dyes is helpful in this regard. To study the higher-order nuclear organization of RNA molecules, cells stained with an RNA-selective dye need to be counterstain with a DNA-selective dye, such as Hoechst or DAPI. The findings of CCDs counterstaining in living cells using commercial nucleus tracker DAPI are shown in Fig. 10b. In both MG-63 and 3T3 cells, the blue nucleus area (i.e., emission from DAPI) identifies the bright green and orange fluorescence from the nucleoli. The merged image displays an intriguing feature: The nucleus and nucleolus, two distinct organelles, can be easily identified by their distinct colours. These findings show that the CCDs are useful for co-staining imaging of cells in addition to being outstanding nucleolus trackers. The only live cell RNA staining dye that is sold commercially is SYTO RNASelect. Its counterstaining capability with DAPI shown in Fig. S6 , represents a similar ability like CCDs. However, its utility in biological applications is limited due to high cost, photo-bleaching tendency, and greater selectivity towards fixed cells. The current study effectively addresses these drawbacks and presents an RNA-specific chemical that exhibits improved photostability when staining nucleolus

The CCDs are a promising multicolour fluorescent probe for imaging nucleoli because of their efficient fluorescence, surface charge, and extremely minimal cytotoxicity. There are few research articles on CD synthesis through the utilization of various organic/inorganic chemical sources, as shown in Table 1 . In comparison, the proposed method utilizes only clove bud powder as a carbon precursor without any modifier as a fluorescent probe for multicolour nucleolus imaging. Therefore, CDs derived from clove buds without using any external oxidizing agent have the advantage of low cost, easy availability, simple fabrication process, better fluorescence, and low toxicity compared to other reported CDs.

We have reported, an easy-to-use, inexpensive, and environmentally friendly material and method for the synthesis of CDs using the fruit buds of the medicinal plant (clove). The strategy does not require any additional steps or reagents for doping or passivation except reaction temperature. Five sets of CCDs are synthesized at distinct reaction temperatures ranging from 120 to 200 °C with a 20 °C gap. The effect of reaction temperatures on optical properties such as absorption and fluorescence are investigated and found to be improved at higher reaction temperatures. The cytocompatibility and hemolysis studies of optimized CCDs are performed representing their suitability in biological environments. Interestingly, CDs are capable of glowing RNA-rich nucleoli which is further investigated using RNase digestion test and spectroscopic techniques. The CCDs show better intracellular photostability during prolonged light source irradiation and comparable counterstaining compatibility concerning commercially available (SYTO RNASelect) dye. Therefore, we have explored medicinal plant fruit buds as a precursor for the synthesis of CDs without utilizing any modifier which has been proven as a potential candidate in the fields of multicolour nucleolus imaging with free radical scavenging ability.
